sessions.note: NO_PROGRESS by COMPLETED-C standard. Per [[verify-claims-against-main]], performed concrete caller-side analysis BEFORE testing variants. Found via `grep -rn 'func_8007CBB0' src/ asm/`: ZERO direct calls. Only references are (1) `src/display.c:710` — the deployment site with stub+asmfix bridge; (2) `asm/funcs/func_8007CBB0.s` — target asm; (3) `asm/data/7D920.data.s:24007` — function pointer entry `.word func_8007CBB0` inside dispatch table D_8009BE2C. Searched D_8009BE6C/D_8009BE2C callers: e.g. `func_8007B4D0` (asm/text2.s:215-232) loads `D_8009BE6C` → table base in `$v0`, then `lw $a0, 0xC($v0)` (slot 3 = func_8007CBB0) and `lw $v0, 0x8($v0)` (slot 2 = func_8007D3F8), jalr $v0 — meaning the caller passes func_8007CBB0 as a FUNCTION POINTER arg0 to a higher-order dispatcher (func_8007D3F8), NOT a direct call. The actual dispatch of slot 3 happens inside whichever dispatchers eventually invoke their fn-ptr arg through the call chain, with arg1's value composed from caller chains and bitfield-packed at runtime. The round-11 hypothesis — `if all callers pass arg1 as a sign-bit-only value (0 or 0x80000000), the inline sign-extract becomes compile-time-known` — is structurally REFUTED because (a) there is no direct caller to inspect, (b) the indirect-fn-ptr dispatchers pass through whatever bit pattern their own callers supply, (c) arg1's full s32 value is consumed at BOTH `((arg1 >> 31) << 10) | 0xE1000000` (sign extract for GP0 0xE1 dithering flag) AND `(arg1 & 0xFFFFFF) | 0x60000000` / `| 0x02000000` (low-24-bits as a GP0 color/command parameter) — so arg1 is genuinely a packed 32-bit value, NOT a pure sign-bit flag. Any signature change that narrows arg1 (e.g. to u8 / s8 / 'flag + color' split args) would lie about the ABI; cheat-reviewer would FAIL on signature-lying.
